Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a study routine. I'd maybe sit down once or twice a week when I felt like it, usually late at night when the apartment was quiet. I'd listen to some lectures while doing other things, like cooking or scrolling through my phone. At 26, I've kind of figured out that forcing myself into rigid schedules just doesn't work for me, so I kept things loose.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place, really. Sometimes I wouldn't touch anything for weeks, then I'd have a burst of activity when I remembered something was due. Deadlines were the main driver, but even then I often missed them. I had other things going on in my life that felt more pressing than coursework most of the time.
Q3: Usually it was either a notification email about something due or just boredom, to be honest. Sometimes I'd log in just to see what was happening in the discussion forums, not necessarily to study. The gamified tasks they introduced actually got me curious a few times, which was surprising because I usually don't care about that stuff.
Q4: Not really, no. It never became a habit for me. There were maybe a couple of weeks where I logged in more regularly because of those gamified challenges they introduced, but that faded pretty quickly. I just couldn't make myself care enough to build it into my daily life.
Q5: The video lectures were actually decent because I could listen to them while doing other things. I'm an auditory learner, so just hearing someone explain concepts works better for me than reading dense text. The discussion posts were sometimes interesting too, especially when other students had different perspectives on the material.
Q6: I basically ignored most of the readings and the written assignments. They felt tedious and I kept telling myself I'd get to them later, but later never came. The quizzes I'd skip because I figured I could cram before the exam instead, which obviously didn't work out great for me.
Q7: Something had to feel either interesting or immediately useful for me to bother with it. If I couldn't see how it connected to anything real or if it felt like busywork, I'd just skip it. The gamified tasks were actually clever because they made even boring content feel like less of a chore, at least for a while.
Q8: Yeah, optional stuff was basically invisible to me. If it wasn't required, my brain just categorized it as 'not my problem.' Required activities I'd at least think about doing, even if I ended up not doing them. There's something about the lack of consequences with optional work that made it easy to dismiss.
Q9: Definitely. I downloaded a bunch of lecture recordings and would listen to them on my commute or while working out. I also had some textbooks and would occasionally flip through them, though not as much as I should have. A lot of my studying happened away from my computer entirely.
Q10: There were times I'd be thinking through concepts while walking around or talking things through with a friend who's in a similar field. None of that shows up anywhere. I'd also replay lectures in my head while doing mundane tasks. It's not structured studying, but it's still processing the material in some way.
Q11: Yeah, all the time. I'd download a video or PDF once and then never open the LMS version again. I preferred having things locally so I didn't need internet or have to deal with the platform. My offline engagement was probably higher than my online activity suggested.
Q12: Around midterms I just completely checked out. I had some personal stuff happening and the course felt like the lowest priority. It wasn't that the material got harder necessarily, I just couldn't bring myself to care when other parts of my life were demanding attention. My motivation, which was already low, basically hit zero.
Q13: Competing responsibilities were the big one. I've got a part-time job and a social life, and honestly those felt more important than assignments I wasn't interested in. Confusion played a role too—when I didn't understand something, I'd just avoid it rather than seek help. It was easier to disengage than struggle.
Q14: Oh, plenty of times. I'd have a video playing but I'd be doing something else entirely, not really absorbing anything. Or I'd open readings and skim them without actually processing the content. My eyes were on the material but my brain was elsewhere. It was going through the motions.
Q15: Constantly. I knew assignments were important but I'd tell myself I'd do them tomorrow, and then tomorrow would come and I'd push it again. Part of it was just not caring enough, part of it was avoiding the discomfort of actually having to focus and work. I'm pretty resilient emotionally, so the guilt didn't really get to me much.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to do anything, and even then I missed most of them. I'd see a deadline approaching and think 'okay, time to actually look at this,' but by then it was usually too late to do quality work. My engagement was completely reactive rather than proactive.
Q17: Not really. By the time feedback came, I'd already mentally moved on from that assignment. I rarely went back to look at comments or corrections. It felt like old news. Maybe if feedback came faster I would have cared more, but the delay made it feel irrelevant to what I was currently supposed to be doing.
Q18: The course structure was pretty standard, which meant it was easy to ignore. Weekly modules, assignments, discussions—nothing that really grabbed my attention or made me feel like I needed to participate. The gamified elements were a nice surprise and actually got me more involved than I expected, but the overall structure didn't help my engagement.
Q19: The gamified tasks actually made things easier because they broke stuff into smaller, more manageable pieces with immediate rewards. But the heavy reading requirements and long written assignments made engagement harder. I needed more audio and interactive content, less walls of text that I'd just avoid.
Q20: I think that's me, honestly. I'd log in, watch some videos, participate in discussions a bit, but when it came to actually submitting work, I just couldn't follow through. The barrier between consuming content and producing work is huge. Browsing and clicking feels easy; actually doing assignments requires effort I wasn't willing to give.
Q21: That makes sense to me. Some people are efficient—they know exactly what they need, they get it, they study offline, and they perform. I'm kind of the opposite. I was moderately active online but it didn't translate to results because I wasn't using that time effectively. Activity doesn't equal learning.
Q22: Logs can show when you clicked on things and how long pages were open, but they can't tell if you were actually paying attention. They miss all the offline studying, the thinking, the conversations with peers outside the platform. They also can't measure understanding or genuine interest—just surface-level interaction.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and wanting to understand it, not just going through the motions. It's when you're curious and the material feels relevant to your life somehow. For me, I wasn't really engaged this semester—I was just present sometimes, which isn't the same thing.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show that I actually found some of the content interesting when I bothered to pay attention. It wouldn't show the conversations I had with friends about the topics, or the thinking I did while commuting. It also wouldn't show that my disengagement wasn't about the course being bad—it was about where I was at in my life and my priorities.
